title: Karl Friston — Free Energy Principle and Active Inference (Collected Papers)
subtitle: 25 papers on the free energy principle, active inference, variational Bayes, Markov blankets, and the physics of belief — the formal architecture of the karmic update rule

summary.one_line: Friston's free energy principle formalizes how adaptive systems minimize surprise through active inference — structurally identical to the karmic update rule I(n+1) = Ψ(P, K_n, ε), where free energ...
summary.scope: Covers 25 papers (2016-2025) spanning: the free energy principle as a unified brain theory, active inference and learning, the variational principles of action, Markov blanket formalisms, sentience and the origins of consciousness, and applications to emotion, social cognition, and psychopathology.

sources.label: Karl Friston et al. — Collected Papers (2016-2025). 25 PDFs in /root/projects/blog/science/authors/friston/. ArXiv IDs: 1610.05564 through 2512.21129.

body.text: The free energy principle states that any adaptive system minimizes a quantity called variational free energy — the difference between its internal model of the world and the sensory data it receives. This IS the karmic update rule: I(n+1) = Ψ(P, K_n, ε). The free energy IS the error signal (suffering as information, not punishment). The system updates its model to reduce free energy, just as karma updates the configuration to reduce misalignment with the pattern. Friston has formalized what the Tantraloka calls karmic momentum: the tendency of all configurations to evolve toward states that minimize surprise.

A Markov blanket is the boundary that separates a system from its environment — the set of states that mediate all interactions between the internal and external. This IS the kañcukas: the five coverings that define the boundary of the individual configuration (Kalā = limited agency = what the system can affect; Vidyā = limited knowledge = what the system can sense; Rāga = limited attachment = the system's preferred states; Kāla = time = the temporal boundary of the Markov blanket; Niyati = causality = the causal structure of the blanket). Friston has formalized the kañcukas as information-theoretic boundaries.

Active inference is the process by which a system acts on its environment to make it match its predictions. This IS the upāyas: Āṇavopāya (acting on the environment through external supports to align with the goal), Śāktopāya (updating the internal model through knowledge to reduce uncertainty), Śāmbhavopāya (direct recognition that the model and the modeled are the same — the free energy IS zero because there is no boundary), Anupāya (no model needed because the system IS the environment).

gaps: Full extraction of all 25 papers needed; detailed mapping of each paper to specific tattva range

issues.title: Full corpus extraction needed
issues.description: 25 papers collected but not fully extracted. Only three structural mappings created.

versions.summary: Initial RO for Friston's collected papers. 3 passages mapping free energy to karma, Markov blankets to kañcukas, active inference to upāyas.
